exemplar.title: To what extent has human activity affected the characteristics of the Jeziorka river and its valley in the non-urbanised and urbanised course?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has human activity affected the characteristics of the Jeziorka river and its valley in the non-urbanised and urbanised course?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear, testable fieldwork question with explicit theoretical grounding
Precise geographic context with feasibility for primary data collection
Highâquality locational maps following mapping conventions
weaknesses: Literature citations lack complete reference details
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands achieved the topâband descriptors: the question is geographical and hypothesisâdriven, the location is precisely defined and feasible, and mapping conventions are fully met. This merits the maximum 3 marks for Criterion A.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Comprehensive explanation of methods tied to hypotheses
Correct implementation of stratified sampling and velocity measurement procedures
Strong theoretical alignment through use of standard formulae
weaknesses: Limited spatial points reduce overall data reliability
Procedure description omits details (float weight/shape, variable definitions)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method description fully meets the top descriptor, but limitations in data quantity (only two sites) prevent the dataâcollection strand from reaching band 3. Overall, the work sits firmly in band 2 (methods sound but minimal data), so 2 marks are awarded.

strengths: All collected data directly linked to hypotheses
Presentation techniques (tables, charts, photos) are generally appropriate
weaknesses: Narrow spatial coverage limits sufficiency for full analysis
Inconsistencies in captions and bibliography formatting impede clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While data are relevant and presentations appropriate, the restricted spatial scope and recurring formatting errors place the work at the lower end of the mid band. This justifies 2 marks rather than a higher mark in Criterion C.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Logical linking of nitrate trends to agricultural runoff
weaknesses: No inferential statistical testing or confidence intervals
Gaps in depth/width data and slope measurements limit explanatory depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Descriptive techniques and data interpretation align with band 2 descriptors, but the absence of any inferential statistics and notable gaps in evidence prevent progression to higher bands. Therefore, 2 marks are awarded for Criterion D.

strengths: Clear, succinct conclusion revisiting each hypothesis
Conclusions fully supported by numerical results
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion restates findings with numerical evidence and is fully supported by the analysis, fulfilling the topâband descriptor for Criterion E and meriting 2 marks.

strengths: Identification of both methodological strengths (replicate trials, stratified sampling) and limitations (seasonality, limited sites)
Relevant suggestions for improvement (increasing sampling sites, adding depth/width measurements)
weaknesses: Lacks depth in discussing the impact of suggested improvements (e.g., sensor calibration, temporal bias)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation outlines pertinent strengths and weaknesses and offers valid improvement suggestions, but the explanations of potential impact are not fully developed. This aligns with the midâband descriptor, justifying 2 marks for Criterion F.

strandSelections.reasoning: Several locational maps include scale, north arrow, colour key and annotated human/physical features.  Although hand-edited, they respect mapping conventions and clearly situate both study sites, satisfying the highest descriptor.

The fieldwork question is clearly geographical, explicitly focuses on human impacts on fluvial characteristics and is framed by two testable hypotheses.  The student links the question to recognised concepts such as channelisation, dams and pollution, demonstrating theoretical grounding that allows measurable hypotheses (velocity and chemical concentrations).  This alignment with syllabus ideas (human modification of rivers) satisfies the top-band requirement.

Tables, bar charts, annotated photos and maps constitute appropriate presentation techniques; they are clear but not optimised through statistical graphics or GIS overlays, placing the work in the 3-4 mark band.

Techniques (float trials, reagent kits) are correctly implemented with multiple repeats, yet the data set is limited to only two spatial points, restricting reliability.  Hence the work meets band 2 where methods are sound but data volume remains minimal.

Mean velocity and percentage-increase calculations are accurate and appropriate, yet no inferential statistics (e.g., t-tests) or confidence limits are used.  This corresponds to the 3-4 mark descriptor of appropriate descriptive/statistical techniques.

The student explains how slope, channelisation and agricultural runoff might shape observed patterns, linking most trends and anomalies back to the hypotheses.  However, the discussion is not exhaustive, so the 3-4 band is the best fit.

A very specific stretch of the Jeziorka River is pinpointed; maps, photographs and textual description distinguish a middle-course forest site from a lower-course channelised site.  Accessibility and feasibility for primary data collection are stated and justified, so the geographic context is precise and appropriate for the investigation.

The evaluation notes strengths (replicate trials, stratified sampling) and several limitations (seasonality, limited sites) but does not explore every stage or methodological bias in depth.  This is an appropriate outline rather than a comprehensive explanation.

All collected measurements (time trials, chemical concentrations) directly address the hypotheses, but the narrow spatial coverage leaves some analytical gaps.  This aligns with âmost data relevant allowing partial analysisâ, the mid-band.

The conclusion succinctly revisits each hypothesis and cites numerical evidence (percentage changes, velocity means).  It is consistently derived from earlier analysis, fulfilling the âclear and fully supportedâ criteria.

Analysis covers both variables and provides some explanatory depth, but omits depth/width data and significance testing, leaving identifiable gaps.  Thus it achieves the requirements of analysis with some omissions (band 3-4).

The student gives a step-by-step account of velocity measurement, stratified sampling rationale, chemical-testing protocol and formulae used.  Each method is explicitly tied to a hypothesis, demonstrating comprehensive explanation and theoretical alignment.
Titles, units, keys and significant figures are usually correct; minor spelling and figure-referencing issues occur but do not impede comprehension.  Occasional errors fit the middle band.

Suggestions such as measuring depth/width, increasing sites and sampling in spring are relevant and linked to expected improvements, yet the discussion of their potential impact is brief.  This matches the mid-band descriptor for improvement suggestions.

annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The bibliography shows inconsistent formatting (italics, punctuation); adhere to a standardized citation style.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Is the Othman et al. (2017) flow method properly cited and included in the bibliography? Please verify.
The question clearly frames the investigation, linking human activity to fluvial characteristics and allowing for testable hypotheses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: To sharpen focus, the question could specify which river characteristics (e.g. velocity, chemical concentrations) are under investigation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The theoretical context for hypotheses is well-grounded, but the literature citations would benefit from complete reference details.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 1 is directional and grounded in theory, facilitating clear, measurable testing.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 2 explicitly identifies chemical parameters, providing clear criteria for comparison between courses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The geographic context precisely situates the Jeziorka River with administrative and physiographic details, enabling feasible primary data collection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The map includes scale, north arrow and annotated features, adhering well to mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Satellite imagery effectively contrasts non-urbanised and urbanised sites, supporting spatial representation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The stratified sampling method is logically explained with clear linkage to the research objectives.
The velocity measurement procedure lacks mention of float weight or shape control, which could affect consistency across trials.
Multiple trials (10) and dual-bank timing strengthen the reliability of the velocity data.
Label steps directly on the procedure figure and reference them in the text to integrate visual and description.
Inclusion of standard velocity formulas from credible sources demonstrates strong theoretical alignment.
Although formula is correct, explicitly define all variables and units to avoid ambiguity.
The choice of water quality parameters aligns well with the hypothesis and methodological aims.
The percentage increase formula is applied correctly, enabling quantitative comparison of reagent levels.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Include error bars or standard deviation on the bar chart to reflect data variability and support conclusions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The interpretation asserts âno significant impactâ but lacks statistical testing; incorporate a t-test or non-parametric alternative.
Proposed influence of slope is plausible but no gradient measurements are presented; integrate slope data or DTM analysis.
Annotate the figure showing float paths with scale bars and labels to validate path length comparisons.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Interpretation linking nitrate increase to agricultural runoff shows strong geographical reasoning.
Concerns about kit calibration and detection limits are valid; include details on kit sensitivity and error margins.
Explanation for stable ammonia and nitrite levels reflects understanding of biogeochemical cycles.
The conclusion succinctly revisits findings for both hypotheses and is fully supported by analysis.
The evaluation acknowledges depth/width measurement as improvement but could discuss sensor calibration errors or temporal sampling bias.
Reflecting on seasonality demonstrates critical depth in evaluating temporal limitations of the study.
Detail the proposed systematic sampling design (e.g. transect spacing, interval) to illustrate feasibility and strengthen reliability.
Access dates for online sources should be uniform in format (e.g. DD MMM YYYY) to maintain consistency.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
